settings.title: Spring Rose Tasting, One Evening Only
settings.excerpt: Six roses from Provence to Tavel, poured blind, with snacks to match, on the last Thursday of April.
settings.body:
Rose season is nearly here, so we are clearing the back tables for a guided tasting on the last Thursday of April.

You will try six bottles blind, from a pale Provence to a structured Tavel that drinks more like a light red. We will reveal each one after you guess, and there is no wrong answer.

Why we pour it blind

Tasting without the label is the quickest way to drop your assumptions. A pale pink wine looks delicate, so people expect it to taste that way, and a darker rose can surprise you with real grip and depth. Cover the bottles and you taste what is actually in the glass rather than what the colour or the region tells you to expect. By the end of the evening most people find their idea of what rose can be has stretched a good deal.

We walk through them in a deliberate order, from the lightest and driest toward the fuller, more structured pours, so each one has a fair hearing.

What the evening includes

  • Six roses poured blind, revealed one at a time after you guess.
  • A board of olives, cured ham, and marcona almonds to match.
  • A relaxed walk-through of why each wine tastes the way it does.

Tickets are thirty-five and include the snacks. Seats are limited to sixteen, so reserve at the bar or by phone. It tends to fill within a few days once word gets around, so do not leave it to the last week.
